attributes.stem:
A 56-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of worsening shortness of breath. He has difficulty walking down to the local shops. He has established interstitial lung disease. He does not have a cough or fever. A computed tomography scan four months ago showed honeycomb changes in the basal lung fields, and recent pulmonary function tests show a restrictive pattern. He takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears mildly dyspnoeic at rest. His pulse rate is 86/min, blood pressure 130/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 91% on room air, and temperature 36.9°C. Inspiratory crackles can be heard at both lung bases. His oxygen saturation levels do not change when he walks.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management strategy?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with gradually worsening dyspnoea due to progression of his interstitial lung disease (ILD). The absence of fever, cough, or acute deterioration suggests this is disease progression rather than an acute exacerbation.

Pulmonary rehabilitation is the most appropriate management strategy for this patient. It includes exercise training, education, and behavioural interventions designed to improve physical and psychological condition. Multiple studies have demonstrated that pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity, reduces dyspnoea, and enhances quality of life in patients with ILD. This non-pharmacological approach is particularly valuable when pharmacological options are limited or have not shown significant symptomatic benefit.

Azithromycin is an antibiotic with both antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. While it may be used in some chronic respiratory conditions, there is no evidence of infection in this patient (no fever or productive cough). Antibiotics are only indicated when there is clinical evidence of a respiratory infection causing an acute exacerbation of ILD, which is not the case here.

Long-term home oxygen therapy requires formal assessment with arterial blood gas analysis. It is typically indicated when PaO2 is consistently ≤55 mmHg at rest, or ≤60 mmHg with evidence of cor pulmonale or polycythaemia. This patient's SpO2 of 91% (roughly equivalent to a PaO2 of about 60-65 mmHg) and stable oxygen levels with exertion suggest he does not meet criteria for long-term oxygen therapy at this time.

Prednisolone and other corticosteroids have limited evidence of benefit in most forms of ILD, particularly in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (suggested by the honeycomb changes). Corticosteroids carry significant side effects with long-term use and are generally reserved for specific ILD subtypes with inflammatory components or acute exacerbations, neither of which is evident in this case.

Pirfenidone is an antifibrotic medication approved for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. While it may slow disease progression by reducing the rate of decline in forced vital capacity, it has not been shown to improve symptoms or quality of life significantly. Additionally, it requires specialist prescription and monitoring for side effects. While it might be considered as part of this patient's long-term management plan, pulmonary rehabilitation offers more immediate symptomatic benefit.
learningPoints: Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity and quality of life in patients with interstitial lung disease.
Antibiotics are only indicated for infectious exacerbations of interstitial lung disease, not for disease progression.
Long-term oxygen therapy requires formal assessment with arterial blood gas analysis and is indicated when PaO2 is consistently ≤55 mmHg.
references: Mikolasch TA, Garthwaite HS, Porter JC. Update in diagnosis and management of interstitial lung disease. Clin Med 2017; 17:146-53. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28365626/
Maher TM. Interstitial Lung Disease: A Review. JAMA. 2024 May 21;331(19):1655-1665. doi: 10.1001/jama.2024.3669. PMID: 38648021. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38648021/

Althobiani MA, Russell AM, Jacob J, Ranjan Y, Folarin AA, Hurst JR, Porter JC. Interstitial lung disease: a review of classification, etiology, epidemiology, clinical diagnosis, pharmacological and non-pharmacological treatment. Front Med (Lausanne). 2024 Apr 18;11:1296890. doi: 10.3389/fmed.2024.1296890. PMID: 38698783; PMCID: PMC11063378. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38698783/
